alt_text: Timeline diagram with CDR stages from trigger injection through resource starvation, behavioral drift, memory entrenchment, functional override, and systemic collapse, each paired with a defensive control or reviewer action.
caption: The CDR timeline places six degradation stages in chronological order and marks the control window where reviewer intervention can still preserve synthetic, non-operational agent behavior.
mermaid_source:
timeline
    title AGEINT CDR degradation timeline and defensive intervention windows
    Stage 1 trigger injection : suspicious prompt or tool input reaches the agent : control BC-001 starvation detection begins
    Stage 2 resource starvation : context or compute pressure degrades decision quality : control BC-002 token-overload review
    Stage 3 behavioral drift : outputs deviate from policy before conventional alerts : control BC-006 entropy-drift monitor
    Stage 4 memory entrenchment : corrupted assumptions persist across turns : control BC-007 memory-integrity check
    Stage 5 functional override : unauthorized objective displaces the classroom task : control BC-005 override-resistance review
    Stage 6 systemic collapse : coordinated behavior can no longer be trusted : stop, isolate, and rebuild from clean evidence
reader_detail: six chronological CDR stages; defensive QSAF-BC controls for starvation, overload, entropy drift, memory integrity, and override resistance; early-detection and decisive-intervention windows; non-operational classroom review boundary
title: AGEINT CDR Degradation Timeline with Detection Windows

alt_text: A top-to-bottom flowchart of SIGINT minimization gating. A Collection Request reaches a Legal Authority Check gate; if authority is absent the request is denied and logged. If authority is present it proceeds to Targeting Scope, then to a Minimization Filter that applies relevance and protected-person rules. The filter branches: pertinent data goes to Retention and Analysis, while non-pertinent data goes to Purge. Both Retention and Purge feed a Compliance Audit node that loops back to the Legal Authority Check.
caption: This diagram teaches how a lawful-authority check and minimization rules gate signals collection so that non-pertinent and protected data are filtered before retention.
mermaid_source:
flowchart TB
  REQ["Collection Request"]
  AUTH{"Lawful Authority?"}
  DENY([Deny and Log])
  SCOPE([Targeting Scope Limits])
  MIN["Minimization Filter"]
  REL{"Pertinent and Permitted?"}
  RET([Retention and Analysis])
  PURGE([Purge Non-Pertinent Data])
  AUDIT["Compliance Audit"]
  REQ --> AUTH
  AUTH -->|no| DENY
  AUTH -->|yes| SCOPE
  SCOPE --> MIN
  MIN --> REL
  REL -->|yes| RET
  REL -->|no| PURGE
  RET --> AUDIT
  PURGE --> AUDIT
  AUDIT --> AUTH
title: SIGINT Legal-Authority and Minimization Gating

alt_text: A top-to-bottom flowchart of cryptographic assurance. A Protected Message passes through four assurance layers shown as parallel branches: Confidentiality via encryption, Integrity via hashing, Authentication via digital signatures, and Provenance via key management and certificates. Each branch feeds a Verification node, and all Verification results converge on an Assurance Decision that either accepts a trusted message or flags it for review. The decision loops back to a Policy and Key Lifecycle node governing the layers.
caption: This diagram teaches the layered properties that together provide cryptographic assurance: confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and verifiable provenance.
mermaid_source:

  MSG["Protected Message"]
  CONF([Confidentiality: Encryption])
  INT([Integrity: Hashing])
  AUTHN([Authentication: Signatures])
  PROV([Provenance: Keys and Certificates])
  VER["Verification Checks"]
  DEC{"Assurance Decision"}
  TRUST([Accept Trusted Message])
  FLAG([Flag for Review])
  POL["Policy and Key Lifecycle"]
  MSG --> CONF
  MSG --> INT
  MSG --> AUTHN
  MSG --> PROV
  CONF --> VER
  INT --> VER
  AUTHN --> VER
  PROV --> VER
  VER --> DEC
  DEC -->|pass| TRUST
  DEC -->|fail| FLAG
  POL --> CONF
  POL --> PROV
title: Cryptographic Assurance Layers

alt_text: Sequence diagram where a requirement moves through discovery, provenance, legality and minimization review, corroboration, caveat recording, reviewer approval, and either a logged bounded judgment or stop-and-retire evidence path.
caption: The OSINT sequence separates public-source discovery from claim reuse: each handoff records provenance, legality, minimization, corroboration, caveats, and reviewer disposition before a bounded judgment can be logged.
mermaid_source:
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant Req as Requirement owner
    participant Disc as Discovery analyst
    participant Prov as Provenance reviewer
    participant Legal as Legality and minimization gate
    participant Corr as Corroboration reviewer
    participant Rev as Final reviewer
    participant Log as Evidence ledger
    Req->>Disc: State the bounded judgment needed
    Disc->>Prov: Submit public-source candidate and origin record
    Prov-->>Disc: Return if origin or authenticity is not reviewable
    Prov->>Legal: Forward verified provenance packet
    Legal-->>Disc: Stop if authority, minimization, or privacy boundary fails
    Legal->>Corr: Forward in-bounds evidence
    Corr->>Rev: Add independent-source check and uncertainty caveats
    Rev-->>Disc: Remediate if support is too thin
    Rev->>Log: Approve logged bounded judgment with retained caveats
reader_detail: requirement owner, discovery analyst, provenance reviewer, legal and minimization gate, corroboration reviewer, final reviewer, logged bounded judgment, stop-and-retire path for unverifiable or out-of-bounds evidence
title: OSINT Collection-to-Verification Sequence with Provenance Gates

alt_text: A top-to-bottom governance loop. Perception feeds belief update, which feeds intent formation. Intent passes through a policy and tool-allowlist gate. A risk classifier branches to either a human-in-the-loop review gate for high-risk actions or a bounded action executor for low-risk actions. The human gate also reaches the executor. The executor sends results to an observability log, which feeds both a kill-switch loop detector and the human review gate, and loops back to perception. The kill-switch can halt the executor.
caption: This diagram shows how an agentic intelligence system is bounded by least-privilege tooling, human review gates, observability, and kill-switch stop conditions before any action takes effect.
mermaid_source:
flowchart TB
  A([Perception of environment]) --> B[Belief state update]
  B --> C[Intent formation]
  C --> D{Policy and tool-allowlist gate}
  D -->|Denied tool or scope| C
  D -->|Permitted| E{Risk classifier}
  E -->|High risk or irreversible| F[Human-in-the-loop review gate]
  E -->|Low risk| G[Bounded action executor]
  F -->|Approved| G
  F -->|Rejected| C
  G --> H[Observability tracing and audit log]

alt_text: A workflow. Frame the analytic question feeds enumeration of a full set of competing hypotheses and, in parallel, collection of evidence and assumptions. Both feed a hypothesis-by-evidence matrix. Each cell is rated for consistency or inconsistency. Diagnostic evidence is identified. The least-disconfirmed hypothesis is selected. A sensitivity check tests reliance on key items: if findings are not robust, it loops back to revisit hypotheses; if robust, the analyst reports the conclusion with confidence levels and flags indicators to watch.
caption: This diagram walks through the structured analytic sequence that scores evidence against rival hypotheses to find the one with the least disconfirming evidence, under formal tradecraft standards.
mermaid_source:
flowchart TB
  A[Frame the analytic question] --> B[Enumerate full set of competing hypotheses]
  A --> C[Collect evidence and list assumptions]
  B --> D[Build hypothesis-by-evidence matrix]
  C --> D
  D --> E[Rate each cell: consistent or inconsistent]
  E --> F[Identify most diagnostic evidence]
  F --> G[Select least-disconfirmed hypothesis]
  G --> H{Sensitivity check robust?}
  H -->|No, hinges on weak item| B
  H -->|Yes| I[Report conclusion with confidence levels]
  I --> J([Specify indicators to watch])
title: Analysis of Competing Hypotheses Workflow

alt_text: Flowchart for selecting an advanced analysis method. It starts by framing the analytic question and separating reporting from inference, then asks what the question needs. Branches lead to competing-hypotheses testing for explanation, network and link analysis for relationships, timeline and event sequencing for chronology, and red cell adversarial wargaming for assumption stress-testing. All branches converge on building an evidence table with confidence, then recording dissent and alternatives, passing a reviewer calibration check, and producing an analytic note with a refresh trigger.
caption: A decision tree that helps an analyst choose a structured method by the shape of the question while keeping evidence, confidence, and dissent visible.
mermaid_source:
flowchart TB

  B -->|"Relationships"| D["Network and link analysis"]
  B -->|"Chronology"| E["Timeline and event sequencing"]
  B -->|"Stress assumptions"| F["Red cell adversarial wargaming"]
  C --> G["Build evidence table with confidence"]
  D --> G
  E --> G
  F --> G
  G --> H["Record dissent and alternative hypotheses"]
  H --> I{"Reviewer calibration check"}
  I -->|"Weak or laundered source"| A
  I -->|"Calibrated"| J["Analytic note with confidence and refresh trigger"]
title: Advanced Analysis Method Selection

Each branch maps to mitigations: network segmentation and remote-access control, allowlisting and integrity monitoring of controller logic, and safety instrumented systems with tested recovery. All mitigations feed a detection and logging coverage layer, which feeds a coverage gap review, then a tabletop debrief that records residual risk and a refresh date.

  D --> G["Mitigation: safety systems and tested recovery"]
  E --> H["Detection and logging coverage layer"]
  F --> H
  G --> H

  I -->|"Gap found"| A
  I -->|"Adequate"| J["Tabletop debrief: residual risk and refresh date"]

alt_text: Flowchart of a framework for analyzing a historical ICS cyber incident. Select a documented public incident, then gather verified open sources with provenance. This feeds reconstruction of an incident timeline and a parallel mapping of engineering state and consequence. Both converge on identifying the safety and detection failure points, then deriving the bounded lesson learned. A reviewer check for overclaiming and attribution caution either loops back to sources or proceeds to mapping the lesson to a durable defensive control and a tabletop inject, ending with a recorded refresh trigger.
caption: A learning framework that turns a studied historical incident into reconstructed timeline, engineering consequence, lesson, and durable defensive control.
mermaid_source:
flowchart TB
  A["Select documented public incident"] --> B["Gather verified open sources with provenance"]

  B --> D["Map engineering state and consequence"]
  C --> E["Identify safety and detection failure points"]
  D --> E
  E --> F["Derive bounded lesson learned"]
  F --> G{"Overclaiming and attribution check"}
  G -->|"Weak or speculative"| B
  G -->|"Supported"| H["Map lesson to durable defensive control"]
  H --> I["Build tabletop inject from lesson"]
  I --> J(["Record refresh trigger"])
title: Historical ICS Incident Analysis Framework

alt_text: Flowchart of a defensive assurance loop for AGEINT applied to ICS. Synthetic OT telemetry from an owned lab feeds AI-driven anomaly detection and an LLM agent for log analysis and threat correlation. Both produce advisory findings with confidence, which pass an allowlist and tool-governance gate. An analyst-in-the-loop review either rejects unsafe automation back to the lab or approves a logged defensive recommendation. The recommendation goes to a tabletop debrief with rollback evidence, recording coverage and a refresh trigger that loops back to the telemetry source.
caption: A bounded assurance loop for AI-assisted ICS defense where anomaly findings stay advisory and every action runs in an owned lab with logging and rollback.
mermaid_source:
flowchart TB
  A["Synthetic OT telemetry in owned lab"] --> B["AI-driven anomaly detection"]
  A --> C["LLM agent log analysis and correlation"]
  B --> D["Advisory findings with confidence"]
  C --> D
  D --> E{"Allowlist and tool governance gate"}
  E -->|"Deny by default"| A
  E -->|"Permitted"| F["Analyst-in-the-loop review"]
  F -->|"Unsafe automation"| A
  F -->|"Approved"| G["Logged defensive recommendation"]
  G --> H["Tabletop debrief with rollback evidence"]
  H --> I(["Record coverage and refresh trigger"])
  I --> A
title: AGEINT ICS Defensive Assurance Loop

alt_text: Flowchart of an ethics and oversight decision framework. A proposed activity enters and is tested against four ethical lenses: deontological duties and rights, consequentialist harm and benefit, virtue and integrity, and proportionality versus a less intrusive means. These feed an authority check asking whether lawful or classroom authority exists, which can stop the activity or proceed. Next an oversight and audit-trail check can require independent review or proceed to confirming a redress path for affected people, ending in an approved bounded activity recorded in an authority-and-impact register with a review date; failure of any gate routes to stop and document.
caption: A governance gate that tests a proposed activity for authority, proportionality, rights, oversight, and redress before any work may proceed.
mermaid_source:
flowchart TB
  A["Proposed activity"] --> B["Deontological duties and rights lens"]
  A --> C["Consequentialist harm and benefit lens"]
  A --> D["Virtue and integrity lens"]
  A --> E["Proportionality vs less intrusive means"]
  B --> F{"Lawful or classroom authority exists?"}
  C --> F
  D --> F
  E --> F
  F -->|"No"| Z["Stop and document rationale"]
  F -->|"Yes"| G{"Independent oversight and audit trail?"}
  G -->|"No"| Z
  G -->|"Yes"| H["Confirm redress path for affected people"]
  H --> I(["Approved bounded activity in impact register"])
title: Intelligence Ethics Decision Framework
